metadata.title: Prompt Card Builder: Save the Standard, Not Just the Text

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to convert my own biweekly action-item prompt into a real card, and the review-criteria field is where I keep hand-waving. I can name a purpose and inputs easily, but I don't trust myself to write checkable review conditions or an honest failure mode I actually tested. Walk me through one card for my meeting-notes prompt: (1) draft three review criteria phrased as pass/fail gates, not glances; (2) write one failure mode discovered on messy paraphrased notes, not a clean example; (3) show how the revision-history field should record what each edit fixed.

props.command: I want to convert my own biweekly action-item prompt into a real card, and the review-criteria field is where I keep hand-waving. I can name a purpose and inputs easily, but I don't trust myself to write checkable review conditions or an honest failure mode I actually tested. Walk me through one card for my meeting-notes prompt: (1) draft three review criteria phrased as pass/fail gates, not glances; (2) write one failure mode discovered on messy paraphrased notes, not a clean example; (3) show how the revision-history field should record what each edit fixed.
props.segment: Prompt Card Builder: Save the Standard,

beats.narration_text: She used the same action-item extraction prompt every two weeks. She had refined it through four iterations last autumn. It was good. It lived in a chat from November. She could not find it. She rewrote it from memory. The new version missed the instruction to flag items without a named owner. She did not catch it until a stakeholder asked about an unassigned item from three meetings ago. The prompt was not the problem. Losing it was. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, action-item extraction prompt

props.command: Here are some meeting notes. Can you pull out the action items?

beats.narration_text: A prompt saved in chat history is a text. A prompt card is a text plus a standard. The card has eight fields: purpose, inputs, constraints, format, review criteria, example, failure modes, revision history. The review criteria field is the critical one — it transfers what you check when you use the output. Without it, the next person who uses the card has the prompt but not the standard. They cannot catch what the November version was designed to catch.
beats.new_visual_element: Card vs. chat — text vs. text + standard / eight fields / review criteria transfers the standard not just the prompt
beats.spark_line: Transfer the standard, not just the text.

beats.narration_text: The review criteria field is the one most writers skip and the most important for maintaining quality. It answers: after Claude returns the output, what specifically do you check? For action-item extraction: are all items named as actions not topics? Does every item have an owner or an explicit unassigned flag? Does the count match the number of decisions made? Three checkable conditions. The field converts review from a glance to a gate.
beats.new_visual_element: Review criteria field — most skipped / most important / three checkable conditions / glance to gate
beats.spark_line: Checkable conditions convert a glance to a gate.

beats.narration_text: The failure modes field requires honesty: 'tends to merge action items when notes are paraphrased rather than verbatim.' Not generic — tested on the actual failure. A card without an honest failure mode was tested on a clean example, not an edge case. The tested flag in the index confirms it: last tested date, current status — active or needs review. An untested card is false confidence at the moment of need.
beats.new_visual_element: Failure modes honest — specific not generic / tested on edge case / tested flag / untested = false confidence

beats.narration_text: Fifteen minutes to write the card. Paid back every time the workflow runs. The card is not bureaucracy — it is the same discipline as a standard operating procedure, a style guide, a pattern library. The prompt card is not new. It is those things in a new medium. Don't start from zero next time. The card is how you make sure the next run starts where the November version left off.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — completed prompt card / eight fields / review criteria / failure modes / tested flag / index entry
beats.spark_line: Don't start from zero next time.

beats.narration_text: Turn your next successful prompt into a card. Paste this.

props.command: Turn this prompt into a reusable card with eight fields: (1) Purpose — what problem it solves in one sentence, (2) Inputs — what the user provides, (3) Constraints — what the prompt requires Claude to do or not do, (4) Format — structure and length of output, (5) Review criteria — three specific, checkable conditions for a good output, (6) Example — one clean input/output pair, (7) Failure modes — specific edge cases where it underperforms, (8) Revision history — first version, date. [paste your prompt]

Don't start from zero next time. The card is how you make sure the next run starts where the November version left off. Turn your next successful prompt into a card. Paste this.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Prompt Card Builder: Save the Standard, Not Just the Text] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
